sample.title: Doraemon the Movie: Nobita and the Birth of Japan
sample.plot: Doraemon and friends trying to save a boy name Kukuru and his tribe from Gigazombie who want to change history for his own sake in the ancient times.

categories.label: small tribes living by kin and custom
categories.description: People belong first to a clan or village band, with safety, marriage, and duty governed by elders and inherited custom rather than written law. What you'll see: huts and encampments, councils of elders, shared food and labour, and strangers treated with caution or ritual welcome.

categories.label: wild land pressing close to settlement
categories.description: Human communities occupy only small clearings within a much larger world of forests, mountains, rivers, and untamed coasts that still rules the pace of life. What you'll see: deep woods, caves, rough tracks, sudden storms, animal threats, and villages that seem fragile beside the landscape.

categories.label: stone tools beside early craft skills
categories.description: Daily work depends on simple hand tools, fire, pottery, weaving, and other basic crafts made close to home with little separation between maker and user. What you'll see: chipped blades, hearths, clay vessels, hides and woven cloth, hand-built shelters, and people shaping what they need from wood, bone, and stone.

categories.label: survival tied to hunting and gathering
categories.description: Food comes from the seasons, the movement of animals, and local knowledge of plants, so hunger and plenty are always near each other. What you'll see: foraging, trapping, fishing, shared butchering, stores of roots and berries, and constant attention to weather, water, and game.

categories.label: spirits and marvels woven into nature
categories.description: The world is understood as alive with unseen powers, so mountains, beasts, strange places, and unusual events carry sacred or fearful meaning. What you'll see: reverence toward notable creatures and places, ritual gestures, taboos, omens, and wonders treated as part of the order of things.

categories.label: chiefs holding power through personal loyalty
categories.description: Authority rests in the strength, wisdom, or sacred standing of a local leader rather than in lasting offices or far-reaching state rule. What you'll see: followers gathered around a chief, disputes settled face to face, gifts and promises binding people together, and leadership tested in moments of danger.

categories.label: migration and settlement still remaking the land
categories.description: Peoples are still moving, splitting, and founding new homes, so territory is not fixed and the map of who belongs where remains unsettled. What you'll see: journeys into unknown country, newly cleared living places, rival claims over hunting grounds, and encounters between neighbouring tribes.

categories.label: memory kept by tale and spoken lineage
categories.description: The past survives through storytelling, names, and ritual remembrance because there are no records to fix events in writing. What you'll see: fireside tales, elders reciting origins, warnings preserved as legend, and community identity tied to remembered ancestors.

categories.label: healing mixed with herb lore and rite
categories.description: Illness and injury are met with practical remedies, prayer, and ritual acts, since bodily harm and spiritual danger are not sharply separated. What you'll see: gathered herbs, tending by hand, chants and charms, protective ceremonies, and healers working beside family members.

categories.label: outside powers appearing as uncanny intrusions
categories.description: In a legendary past, anything beyond the tribe's known world can arrive as something frighteningly advanced, godlike, or unnatural against the simplicity of ordinary life. What you'll see: awe before strange devices or powers, sacred and fearful reactions to the unfamiliar, and old ways thrown into confusion by wonders.
